headlines.title: Arrests made after Bay Bridge takeover, Oakland police say
headlines.description: Many arrests were made and more than 70 dirt bikes, vehicles and ATVs were impounded following a Bay Bridge takeover on Sunday, according to the authorities. CHP said a group of dirt bikers and ATVs crossed the Bay Bridge westbound to San Francisco. Shortly after, they crossed the bridge once again, this time eastbound. Authorities said a contingent of officers from CHP San Francisco, CHP Oakland and Oakland police stopped traffic to detain the riders. The riders attempted to run away, leaving their dirt bikes and ATVs behind, according to officials. CHP said Oakland PD arrested two people while 50 to 60 dirt bikes and quads were impounded. Oakland police later said more than 70 ATVs and dirt bikes, along with vehicles, were impounded. They also noted multiple arrests were made. The fire department also said they rescued a person who jumped into the water in relation to the incident.

headlines.title: Mountain View residents to spend another week under Boil Water notice
headlines.description: A Mountain View neighborhood will have to spend another week boiling tap water if they want to use it to drink and cook, the city announced Sunday. City officials said they do not anticipate fully lifting the “Boil Water” restrictions for the affected homes before the weekend of May 9 and May 10. This is due to the latest water tests coming back positive for low levels of coliform bacteria in one area of the neighborhood, according to the city. They added these tests remain negative for E. Coli. Mountain View May 1 Mountain View Apr 30 Mountain View Apr 27 Authorities noted the positive tests appear to be concentrated in the water line serving homes on Drucilla Drive and Carla Court. The water issues at this Mountain View neighborhood started on April 24, when a contractor working on a water main in the neighborhood accidentally let cement slurry contaminate the drinking water line. City officials had told residents on Friday they were able to use tap water to cook and clean, but it needed to be boiled first. On Sunday night, city officials said they will being the process for a “super chlorination” of the pipeline serving the Drucilla Drive and Carla Court area (Drucilla Drive between Leona Lane and Montalto Drive and all of Carla Court). They added this section of the pipeline serves 23 households.

headlines.title: Investigation underway following fatal crash in San Leandro
headlines.description: An investigation is underway following a fatal crash that closed all northbound lanes of I-880 in San Leandro for several hours on Sunday morning, according to the California Highway Patrol. According to CHP, the crash happened on northbound I-880, just south of Davis Street, at around 4:20 a.m. They said at least three vehicles were involved. All lanes were closed until 7:30 a.m., according to CHP. They said the cause of the crash is under investigation.

headlines.title: Woman dead and several hospitalized after multi-vehicle crash in San Jose
headlines.description: A woman died and several others were hospitalized following a multi-vehicle and major injury crash in San Jose on Saturday afternoon, according to authorities. San Jose Police said the crash happened in the area of Blossom Hill Road and Santa Teresa Boulevard just before 4 p.m. Several people were taken to local hospital with varying injuries. Police said one of them, a woman, was pronounced dead at the hospital. NBC Bay Area spoke to Luis Silva, a witness who jumped into action to help pull a man from a burning car. “As soon I got here, the car was on fire, and it was myself and then another officer and another gentleman pulling him out of the car, he was a bloody mess, and then once he was sort of like more coherent, we were able to get him out in the street,” Silva said. The police department asked the public to use alternate routes.

headlines.title: Person dies after crashing into power pole in Morgan Hill
headlines.description: A person died after crashing into a power pole in Morgan Hill on Sunday morning, according to the California Highway Patrol. CHP said the crash happened in the area of Watsonville Road and Hayes Lane at around 8 a.m., when a vehicle hit a power pole and the driver was pronounced dead at the scene, according to authorities. No further details were immediately available.

headlines.title: 8 pedestrians hurt after being hit by vehicle in Oakland
headlines.description: Eight people were injured after being hit by a vehicle in Oakland early Saturday morning, according to the police department. Police said officers were responding to reports of fights near 18th Street and Telegraph Avenue when they received another report regarding a vehicle driving on the sidewalk and striking pedestrians in the 500 block of 18th Street at 1:30 a.m. Authorities said five adults and one juvenile were taken to the hospital and are listed in stable condition. Police added another adult was privately taken to the hospital and is also listed in stable condition. Oakland Police said that when they arrived at the scene, they arrested two people who were running away, and recovered firearms on those people. However, Oakland Police said, by that time the driver had already gotten out of the vehicle and was gone from the scene. The Oakland Fire Department later told NBC Bay Area a total of eight people were injured, and one of them was treated and released at the scene. “It’s very chaotic when you first arrive on scene to one of these incidents where a vehicle has traveled into a crowd of people, and it’s very hard to figure out who is a patient, who is injured,” explained Battalion Chief Anthony Sanders with the Oakland Fire Department. Four hours prior to this incident and a few blocks away, Oakland’s First Fridays monthly festival was happening, and Sanders said lingering crowds in the area presented a challenge for fire crews. “Due to the increased traffic and congestion in the area, it took them a little bit longer to get on scene than normal,” he said. Police clarified that this incident was not part of the official First Fridays event. Venessa McGhee, the Festival Director for Oakland First Fridays, explained that the incident on 18th Street happened outside of the hours and permitted event footprint of the festival. McGhee described this month’s festival as a “positive and well-attended celebration of Oakland’s 174th Birthday.” “We do want to emphasize an ongoing concern: incidents that occur in the broader Uptown/Downtown area, especially outside of our event boundaries and hours—are often incorrectly attributed to Oakland First Fridays,” McGhee added. Several Oaklanders in the area on Saturday told NBC Bay Area they want to see the police, the city, and community groups work together to deter violence and fights in the hours well after the First Friday events. “There should be barricades and they shouldn’t let people drive down the streets,” said Isabella Lawrence of Oakland. “Especially when you have such big crowds like that,” said Charlena Wynn, of Oakland, who was walking with Lawrence. Lawrence said she works downtown and noted that Oakland First Fridays help drive traffic to local businesses. She also said that because of crowds on the streets late at night on the first Friday of the month, she tries to go home quickly after work. “It just feels really unsafe which, is sad because this is our neighborhood and community,” said Wynn, who said in her experience, crowds tend to linger in the streets until many bars close at 2:00 a.m. Police ask anyone with information on this incident to contact their Felony Assault Unit at 510-238-3426. Those who have any videos or photos that could help with the investigation are asked to send them to cidvideos@oaklandca.gov.

headlines.title: Woman who was shot in Vallejo dies after being dropped off at hospital
headlines.description: A woman was shot and killed in Vallejo on Friday, according to the Vallejo Police Department. Police were notified that the victim was dropped off at a hospital in critical condition suffering from at least one gunshot wound by someone who then left the scene at about 5:35 p.m. She was given emergency medical care but died from her injuries, police said. Her identity was withheld until the Solano County Sheriff-Coroner can notify the victim’s next of kin. Her death marks the third homicide in Vallejo in 2026, according to police. Anyone with information about the shooting was asked to contact the Vallejo Police Department at (707) 648-5430. Tips can be left anonymously at 1-800-488-9383.

headlines.title: Oakland's Mosswood Recreation Center opens its doors after 10 years
headlines.description: The Mosswood Recreation Center reopened its doors in Oakland on Saturday, after fire gutted the building in 2016. A cloudy Saturday did not stop neighbors from coming out to the brand new Mosswood Recreation Center at Mosswood Park. The brand-new facility officially opened its doors to the public. The new community center is a two story facility with a social hall, kitchen, classrooms, a maker space, computer lab and improvements to the park. “It’s amazing, it’s so beautiful inside we’re so excited for playing with her and coming to community events,” said Alexis of Oakland. “I could never have imagined that it would turn out this good from what was here before, so even though we never want tragedies like fires to occur in our communities, what we have now is next to nothing that exists right now in the city of Oakland,” Councilmember Carroll Fife said. A fire ten years ago tore through the original building, which was built back in the 50s. “I was saying earlier the Black Panther Party held many events here and I used to bring my children here, and I know the history of Mosswood Park and the Center, and to see this is like the phoenix rising and it’s really a testament to the community and the community’s resilience and the community’s vision,” Mayor Barbara Lee said. Investigators still have not determined what caused the fire in 2016, but both city leaders and neighbors say they are ready to put the past behind and look to the future. “We’re always looking for Mosswood Park to be a hub we live so close we want it to be our community park, we want to play here, we want to spend time with our neighbors, and so I think this is really going to bring the people out,” said Laura of Oakland.

headlines.title: 1 dead after wrong way driver causes multi-vehicle crash in Concord
headlines.description: A person died following a multi-vehicle crash involving a wrong way driver in Concord on Friday night, according to the California Highway Patrol. CHP said officers responded to a crash on northbound I-680, north of Treat Boulevard at around 11:20 p.m. Authorities reported a black Charger was traveling wrong way and collided head on with a Tesla. Several other vehicles also crashed into each other, trying to avoid the original collision, according to officials. CHP said a person in the Tesla was declared deceased and the driver in the Charger was taken to the hospital. They add DUI is suspected but not confirmed.

headlines.title: Napa man convicted in murder case involving former CHP captain
headlines.description: A Napa man has been convicted of murder in a case involving a former CHP captain. On Friday, a jury found Thomas O’Donnell guilty in a murder-for-hire plot spanning three states. Prosecutors said former CHP captain Julie Harding hired O’Donnell to kill her husband, Michael. They say O’Donnell lured Michael to an empty home in Kentucky in 2022 and killed him. Michael’s family said they were pleased by the verdict. Julie Harding died by suicide a few months after Michael’s murder. O’Donnell is set to be sentenced on Monday.

headlines.title: Some East Bay voters will get 2 ballots for the same seat: What to know
headlines.description: Starting Monday, all voters across the Bay Area will get their ballot for California’s June primary. But in Congressional District 14, voters will get another ballot just days later for a special election happening two weeks after that primary. This all stems from a sudden vacancy after longtime Congressman Eric Swalwell resigned following sexual assault allegations, shaking up local politics and triggering a rare special election. Alameda County Supervisor Daniel Haubert says the first ballot, arriving Monday, is for the June primary that will send the top two candidates to November to decide who holds the seat long term. “That will elect the long-term member of Congress,” Haubert said. The second ballot arriving just days later is for a special election two weeks after the primary. That vote will decide who fills the seat right away if someone gets more than 50%. If not, there could be a runoff later this summer. “People are confused about which election should they vote in? Does one nulify the other? Is one more important than the other? Why do we have two elections? Is the most common question,” Haubert said. Officials say the ballots will be different colors to help voters tell them apart. But some say they’ll still need to do their homework. County officials say that special election will cost about $6 million, with the possibility of even more if there’s a runoff.

headlines.title: Mountain View residents can use water again, but with precautions
headlines.description: After a week without drinkable water, Mountain View city officials told residents on Friday they can use water again – but only after taking several steps. The City of Mountain View said those 67 homes near Cuesta Park can finally turn their water back on with precautions. But before using it, the city says people need to flush their plumbing systems. That means running hot water for up to 75 minutes for larger heaters, or until it runs cold, and replacing any water filters. Even after all that, the rule is simple: boil water before drinking or cooking. On Saturday, city leaders said they were hoping the state water resources control board will lift the Boil Water order as early as Sunday if test results show no bacteria. The city says it will notify residents once testing confirms the water is bacteria-free. “They’re saying all kinds of things, we gotta flush and this and that. I will not boil water or anything, I will not do anything, until it’s 100% safe by the state,” said Kathy Bonilla, a neighbor. “That sounds a lot of iffy. I’m not boiling water or taking a shower, I’m waiting until my water is clean and tested and ready to go. I’ve waited this long,” said Leslie Edwards, a neighbor. For others, the change brings at least some relief. “We are able to bathe in it, run the dishwasher, and do laundry, which is a really big deal,” said Norman Kitching, a neighbor. City officials say they’ve been testing water samples all week. During Friday’s meeting, they got approval from the state to move into this next phase. It’s been a difficult stretch for residents. The problem started last Friday, when a contractor working on a water main allowed cement slurry to seep into the system, contaminating it with bacteria. “This made it just very, very difficult, you can’t imagine … you can’t even wash your hair,” Bonilla said. Many say they’ve spent extra money on food, laundry, and basic needs. The city now says residents will receive $92 per day for each day affected. “That’s not enough, because I just went to the store, twice,” Bonilla said. The city says if residents need help flushing their plumbing, they can call for assistance. Officials have also filed a claim against the contractor involved.
